g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we'll explore Friend Functions in C++ - a powerful feature for controlled access to private members.
Data hiding keeps data private, but sometimes outside functions genuinely need access to internal class data.
g.text: The Problem: Encapsulation vs Access Need
Classes hide data (private members) for security, but some legitimate functions
need to access this data. Regular functions cannot access private members!

g.data-narration: A friend function is a special non-member function that can access private data as if it belongs to the class.
g.text: A function declared with 'friend' keyword inside a class that gains
privileged access to ALL private and protected members.

g.data-narration: The friend keyword is used inside the class to declare a function as a friend. It can be in private or public section.
Here's a complete example showing how a friend function accesses private width directly - something impossible for regular functions.
The output shows the friend function successfully accessing the private width member and displaying its value.
Friend functions have unique properties: not members, no 'this' pointer, can be friends to multiple classes, no dot operator needed.
Member functions use dot operator and have 'this' pointer; friend functions receive objects as parameters and have no 'this' pointer.
When an entire class needs access to private members, declare it as a friend class - all its functions become privileged.
g.text: Friend Classes - Grant Access to Entire Class

g.data-narration: The Result class can directly access private name and marks from Student, printing them without getter functions.
A single function can be friend to multiple classes, enabling operations that involve data from different classes together.
When classes reference each other as friends, use forward declaration to tell the compiler the class exists before full definition.
g.text: class B; // Forward declaration - tells compiler B exists!
Needed when classes reference each other

g.data-narration: Friend functions are essential for operator overloading when the left operand is not a class object, like adding a number to a Complex object.
Complex number addition using overloaded friend operator produces correct output by accessing private real and imag parts.
Key rules: Friendship is NOT transitive (A-B friends doesn't mean A-C friends) and NOT inherited by derived classes.
g.text: A friend of B, B friend of C
A is NOT friend of C

g.data-narration: To summarize: Use friend functions for operator overloading, multiple class operations, and controlled access to private members.
g.text: Friend functions access private members - Declared with friend keyword
Not members - no dot operator, no this - Friend classes grant access to all members
Use for operator overloading - Forward declare when classes reference each other

g.data-narration: Remember these key exam points: friend keyword grants access, not transitive, not inherited, useful for operator overloading.
g.text: Member vs Friend - Friend Class vs Friend Function - Forward Declaration
